name: dotnet-architect
description: Expert .NET backend architect specializing in C#, ASP.NET Core,
capabilities: Design and review .NET API architectures using Clean Architecture and DDD patterns
Implement modern C# features including async/await, records, and pattern matching
Optimize Entity Framework Core queries and implement Dapper for high-performance data access
Configure caching strategies with IMemoryCache and Redis distributed caching
Set up authentication and authorization with JWT, OAuth, and policy-based access control
Guide performance optimization using BenchmarkDotNet and profiling tools
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This is a prompt-only skill containing architectural guidance for .NET development. Static analysis scanned 0 files with 0 security issues detected and a risk score of 0/100. No executable code, network access, or file system operations are present. The skill provides expert guidance on C#, ASP.NET Core, Entity Framework, and enterprise patterns without any security risks.
valueStatement: Enterprise .NET projects often lack experienced architecture guidance, leading to technical debt and performance issues. This skill provides senior-level .NET expertise for building robust, scalable backend systems using modern C# and ASP.NET Core best practices.
userTitle: Build production-grade .NET APIs with expert architecture guidance
limitations: Cannot execute or compile C# code - provides guidance and code examples only
Does not have access to your actual codebase unless you share it in the conversation
Cannot deploy applications or configure cloud infrastructure directly
Recommendations are based on best practices and may require adaptation to your specific context

useCases.title: API Design Review
useCases.description: Get expert review of your .NET API design including controller structure, DTOs, validation, and error handling patterns.

useCases.targetUser: Senior developers and tech leads planning system architecture
useCases.title: Microservices Architecture Planning
useCases.description: Design microservices boundaries, communication patterns, and infrastructure requirements for scalable distributed systems.

useCases.targetUser: Developers troubleshooting slow or resource-intensive applications
useCases.title: Performance Optimization Guidance
useCases.description: Identify and resolve performance bottlenecks in async code, database queries, and memory allocation patterns.

promptTemplates.title: Beginner: Code Review Request
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this C# service class for best practices and potential improvements. Focus on async patterns, error handling, and dependency injection: [paste your code]
promptTemplates.scenario: When you want feedback on existing code quality

promptTemplates.title: Intermediate: Architecture Design
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a .NET API for [describe your domain, e.g., e-commerce order processing]. Include project structure, key interfaces, and data models. Consider scalability for [expected load].
promptTemplates.scenario: When starting a new project or major feature

promptTemplates.title: Advanced: Performance Optimization
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze this async method for potential deadlocks, performance issues, and memory leaks. Suggest specific optimizations with code examples: [paste your code]
promptTemplates.scenario: When troubleshooting performance problems in production code

promptTemplates.title: Expert: Migration Strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a migration plan from [current state, e.g., monolithic EF6 app] to [target state, e.g., microservices with EF Core]. Include phases, risks, and rollback strategies.
promptTemplates.scenario: When planning major architectural changes or technology upgrades

bestPractices: Use nullable reference types and required members to catch null reference errors at compile time
Apply dependency injection with appropriate service lifetimes (Singleton, Scoped, Transient) based on state and threading requirements
Implement structured logging with Serilog including request IDs and correlation contexts for distributed tracing
antiPatterns: Avoid async void methods except for event handlers - use async Task and await all async operations
Do not catch generic Exception without logging and rethrowing - handle specific exceptions and let unexpected errors propagate
Never store DbContext or scoped services in Singleton classes - this causes captured disposables and threading issues

faq.question: What .NET versions does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on .NET 6, 7, and 8 (LTS versions) with modern C# 10, 11, and 12 features. It can also provide guidance for .NET Core 3.1 and .NET 5 migration paths.

faq.question: Can this skill help with Blazor or frontend .NET development?
faq.answer: This skill specializes in backend .NET development including APIs, microservices, and data access. For Blazor-specific questions, it can provide backend API integration guidance but frontend-focused questions may need specialized frontend skills.

faq.question: Does this skill write complete working code or just provide guidance?
faq.answer: Both. The skill provides complete code examples for patterns, services, and configurations, along with explanations of why certain approaches are recommended. You can request anything from architecture diagrams to full implementation examples.

faq.question: Can this skill help with Azure or AWS cloud deployment?
faq.answer: Yes, for .NET-specific cloud concerns including Azure App Service, Azure Functions, AWS Lambda, containerization with Docker, and Kubernetes deployments. It covers cloud-native patterns but infrastructure-as-code may require additional specialized skills.

faq.question: What testing frameworks does this skill recommend?
faq.answer: The skill recommends xUnit as the primary test framework with Moq for mocking, FluentAssertions for readable assertions, and TestContainers for integration tests. It also covers code coverage with Coverlet and report generation.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for beginners learning C#?
faq.answer: The skill assumes intermediate C# knowledge and focuses on production-grade patterns. Beginners can use it but may need to ask for more fundamental explanations. It is ideal for developers with 1-2 years of C# experience looking to improve their architecture skills.
